permit__coordinator_information.address: 7421 Lankershim Blvd, Suite 310, North Hollywood, CA 91605
permit__coordinator_information.locations: Primary: Old Cajon Pass Road pullout near Hwy 138, San Bernardino County, CA 92336 (mile marker 7.4). Secondary holding/staging area: 12500 Wagon Train Rd, Phelan, CA 92371 (crew parking and SFX prep only, no live fire).

activity_details.activity_special_fx_materials:
Scene description:
Feature film car chase sequence on Old Cajon Pass Road involving controlled bullet-hit effects on a stationary picture vehicle, low-level gasoline fireball in a ditch approximately 20 feet off the roadway, and atmospheric effects (dust, smoke) to simulate a near-miss explosion and brake lockup. No live firearms will be used; all muzzle flashes are simulated with non-gun pyrotechnic squibs and LED inserts.

Planned activities (step-by-step overview):
1. Morning setup and safety walk-through (no ignition):
   - Establish a 150-foot safety perimeter around the fireball site and 50-foot perimeter around the bullet-hit vehicle.
   - Place and bury fireball pots in a shallow trench in the existing dirt turnout, oriented away from vegetation and crew.
   - Mount bullet-hit squibs on the driver-side door and windshield of the hero vehicle (non-drivable shell), with blast shields for camera and crew.
   - Place atmospheric dust mortars (compressed air with cork/dirt mix) along the shoulder to simulate debris from the near-miss impact.
   - Conduct safety briefing with cast, crew, fire safety officer, and CHP/sheriff if present, including demonstration of audible countdown and emergency stop procedures.

2. Bullet-hit sequence (3–5 takes):
   - Use electric-match–initiated bullet-hit squibs on car body panels and windshield timed to the stunt driver’s braking mark.
   - All actors inside vehicle will wear eye protection between takes and remove only on director’s call immediately before rolling.
   - Clear all non-essential crew to a minimum of 25 feet from the vehicle and behind hard cover (grip truck, concrete barrier, or designated shield).

3. Fireball sequence (2–3 takes):
   - Controlled gasoline-based fireball in a pre-dug dirt trench approximately 20 feet long by 2 feet wide, with fire pans and buried steel manifold.
   - Fireball height expected: 18–22 feet, duration approximately 2–3 seconds.
   - Fire department engine and 1–1/2" charged line on standby at all times; minimum 2,000 gallons water on site.
   - Clear area of all dry brush within a 30-foot radius of the trench; pre-wet surrounding ground as directed by fire safety officer.

4. Atmospheric and brake lockup effects:
   - Use non-pyrotechnic dust hits (compressed-air mortars) loaded with clean playground sand and sifted local dirt to create debris plumes.
   - Apply rosin and water mix on a short section of pavement to help stunt vehicle achieve visible tire smoke during hard braking without excessive speed.

5. Wrap and site restoration:
   - Thorough policing of the area to remove all debris, wiring, and residual materials.
   - Final walk-through with fire safety officer to confirm no smoldering material, hot spots, or environmental impact.

Special effects materials and approximate quantities:
1. Bullet-hit squibs and charges:
   - 18 x body panel hit squibs (pre-manufactured, non-detonating, 1-gram charge equivalent each)
   - 8 x windshield hit squibs with pre-scored safety glass
   - 60 x electric matches for redundancy and multiple takes (only as many as required will be wired at one time)
   - 200 ft of 18-gauge 2-conductor firing cable
   - 2 x 12V isolated firing boxes with safety key and arming indicators

2. Fireball materials:
   - Gasoline: maximum 10 gallons total on site; per take: 3–4 gallons in fire pans/manifold (never exceeding 5 gallons in active use at one time)
   - 4 x steel fire pans (24" x 24" x 6")
   - 1 x welded steel manifold with 4 ports for even flame distribution
   - 2 x propane pilot torches for ignition (small 20 lb cylinders, one active, one spare)
   - Propane: 2 x 20 lb cylinders, secured upright and placed minimum 25 feet from ignition point

3. Atmospheric/dust effects:
   - 6 x compressed-air dust mortars (80–100 psi max)
   - 6 x 5-gallon buckets of sifted dirt/sand mix (no rocks or debris larger than 1/4")
   - 1 x 60-gallon portable air compressor with safety relief valve

4. Fire safety and control equipment (non-pyrotechnic but present on site):
   - 6 x 20 lb ABC fire extinguishers (distributed at car, fireball trench, and camera line)
   - 2 x 2.5-gallon water extinguishers (pressurized water)
   - 10 x fire-resistant wool blankets for actor and crew protection
   - 4 x Class A/B fire beaters

5. Personal protective equipment (PPE):
   - Fire-retardant (FR) wardrobe for all principal cast in close proximity to fireball
   - FR balaclavas, Nomex gloves, and long-sleeve FR base layers for stunt performers
   - Safety goggles or face shields for all crew within 25 feet of any squib or dust mortar
   - Ear protection for all personnel within 50 feet of fireball or multiple simultaneous squib hits

Safety procedures and controls:
- All pyrotechnic devices and materials will be stored in approved Type 3 day boxes when not in active use.
- Only licensed pyrotechnicians (coordinator and listed assistants) will handle, arm, or fire any charges.
- A full safety briefing will be conducted at call time and again prior to any live fire or squib run.
- A 10-second audible countdown will be given before any ignition; last three seconds will be verbalized clearly as "3-2-1-FIRE".
- Wind conditions will be monitored continuously; fireball and dust hits will be suspended if sustained winds exceed 15 mph or if gusts exceed 20 mph, or as otherwise directed by the fire safety officer.
- An emergency shutdown plan has been established: all ignition sources isolated, fuel valves closed, and communication to halt camera and stunt action immediately.

Additional notes:
- No open flames, smoking, or personal lighters will be permitted within the established hot zone.
- No live ammunition or operational firearms will be present on set. All weapons are rubber or non-firing replicas.
- All activities comply with California State Fire Marshal regulations for motion picture pyrotechnics, and a copy of the coordinator’s and assistants’ current State Pyrotechnics licenses will be provided with this form.
- No overnight storage of pyrotechnic materials on location; all remaining materials will be removed from site daily and returned to licensed storage.
activity_details.times_of_activity: Daily pyrotechnic window: 09:30–16:30. Live fireball shots planned between 11:00–15:00 only, with squib and dust effects between 10:00–16:00 as daylight allows.

signatures.production_co_signature: Laura Chen, Line Producer, High Desert Practical FX, LLC
